era_believed.statement: Sherlock Holmes resides at 221B Baker Street in London with his friend and colleague Dr. John Watson.
Professor James Moriarty is the Napoleon of crime and the mastermind behind a vast criminal network in London.
Dr. Watson was wounded in the Battle of Maiwand by a Jezail bullet that struck him in the shoulder.
Mrs. Hudson is the long-suffering landlady of 221B Baker Street who tolerates Holmes's eccentric habits.
Inspector Lestrade of Scotland Yard frequently consults Sherlock Holmes on cases that baffle the official police.
Mycroft Holmes is Sherlock's older brother who possesses even greater deductive powers but lacks the energy to use them.
The Diogenes Club is a London gentleman's club where no member is permitted to speak to any other member.
Irene Adler is the only woman to have ever outwitted Sherlock Holmes, earning his lasting admiration.
Holmes and Moriarty both fell from the Reichenbach Falls during their fatal struggle, though Holmes secretly survived.
Holmes uses a seven percent solution of cocaine injected intravenously when no intellectual stimulation is available.
The Baker Street Irregulars are a group of street urchins employed by Holmes as informants and surveillance agents.
Holmes solved the mystery of the speckled band, revealing that Dr. Roylott used a trained swamp adder to commit murder.
Colonel Sebastian Moran was Moriarty's chief assassin and the second most dangerous man in London after the Professor.
Holmes disguised himself as an elderly Norwegian named Sigerson during the three years he was believed dead.
Watson's literary agent published Holmes's adventures in The Strand Magazine for the British reading public.
The Red-Headed League was a fraudulent organization created to lure Jabez Wilson away from his pawnshop.
Holmes deduced the entire history of Watson's brother from a single pocket watch presented to him for examination.
Professor Moriarty authored a treatise on the dynamics of an asteroid that earned him a university mathematics chair.
Holmes kept his tobacco in the toe of a Persian slipper hanging on the mantelpiece at 221B Baker Street.
The Hound of the Baskervilles was a giant dog coated in phosphorescent paint used to terrify the Baskerville family.
Inspector Gregson is the smartest of the Scotland Yard detectives, though Holmes considers that a low bar.
Holmes preserved his unanswered correspondence by transfixing it to the mantelpiece with a jack-knife.
The five orange pips were a death warning sent by the Ku Klux Klan to John Openshaw and his family.
Irene Adler kept a compromising photograph of herself with the King of Bohemia as insurance against persecution.
Holmes identified forty-two different impressions left by bicycle tyres using his specialized monograph on the subject.
Watson kept his revolver in his desk drawer and brought it along on dangerous cases at Holmes's request.
The Gloria Scott affair was the first case that led young Sherlock Holmes to consider detection as a profession.
Victor Trevor was Holmes's only friend during his university years before he met Dr. Watson in London.
Holmes used his knowledge of 140 different types of tobacco ash to solve several important cases.
The Musgrave Ritual was an ancient family ceremony whose words concealed directions to the lost crown of Charles I.
Holmes once faked his own death for three years to dismantle the remainder of Moriarty's criminal organization.
Billy the page boy served at 221B Baker Street and occasionally assisted Holmes with minor errands and disguises.
Dr. Watson was married to Mary Morstan, whom he met during the case of the Sign of the Four.
Mary Morstan came to Holmes seeking help after receiving anonymous gifts of pearls over several years.
The Copper Beeches case involved a governess hired under suspicious conditions by the sinister Jephro Rucastle.
Holmes demonstrated his mastery of baritsu, a Japanese martial art, during his fight with Moriarty at the Falls.
Mycroft Holmes essentially serves as the British government, coordinating all departments from his Whitehall office.
Holmes kept his bullets in a patriotic V.R. pattern shot into the sitting room wall at Baker Street.
The Yellow Face case is notable because Holmes's deduction proved entirely wrong, a rare instance of failure.
Toby is a half-spaniel mongrel dog used by Holmes to track scents across London during difficult investigations.
Holmes retired to the Sussex Downs to keep bees, leaving behind his career as a consulting detective.
The Valley of Fear involves a secret society called the Scowrers operating in a Pennsylvania coal mining community.
Shinwell Johnson is an ex-criminal who serves as Holmes's informant within London's criminal underworld.
The Bruce-Partington submarine plans were stolen from the Admiralty, leading Mycroft to ask Sherlock for help.
Holmes solved the Naval Treaty case by discovering the document hidden beneath a breakfast serving dish cover.
Charles Augustus Milverton was the worst man in London, a master blackmailer whom Holmes compared to a serpent.
Holmes broke into Milverton's house at Appledore Towers and witnessed his murder by a wronged noblewoman.
The Mazarin Stone is a famous yellow diamond that Holmes recovered from the criminal Count Negretto Sylvius.
Langdale Pike is a gossip columnist who provides Holmes with information about London society scandals.
Holmes authored a monograph upon the tracing of footsteps, including observations on the use of plaster of Paris.
The three Garridebs case was a scheme by a counterfeiter to gain access to a basement printing press.
Holmes solved the disappearance of Silver Blaze, a famous racehorse, by noting the curious incident of the dog.
The dog that did not bark in the night was the key clue that solved the Silver Blaze mystery for Holmes.
Watson's tin dispatch box at Cox and Company bank contains unpublished case notes from Holmes's investigations.
Holmes played the violin with considerable skill, often composing his own pieces while deep in thought.
The Creeping Man case involved a professor who injected himself with monkey serum to regain his youth.
Holmes solved the Mystery of the Dancing Men by deciphering a substitution cipher drawn as stick figures.
Hilton Cubitt was murdered at Ridling Thorpe Manor after receiving threatening messages in the dancing men cipher.
The engineer's thumb was severed by a counterfeiting gang when Victor Hatherley discovered their secret hydraulic press.
Holmes used a wax bust of himself placed in the Baker Street window to draw the fire of Colonel Moran's air rifle.
Colonel Moran attempted to assassinate Holmes using a specially constructed silent air rifle firing revolver bullets.
The Solitary Cyclist case involved a woman being followed on her bicycle rides by a mysterious bearded stranger.
Holmes determined that James Windibank disguised himself as Hosmer Angel to prevent his stepdaughter from marrying.
Lady Frances Carfax was kidnapped and nearly buried alive in an oversized coffin by the villain Holy Peters.
The Sussex Vampire case was solved when Holmes discovered a jealous child had been poisoning the baby with darts.
Holmes catalogued his vast index of criminal cases and personalities in a series of scrapbooks and reference volumes.
The Blue Carbuncle was a famous gemstone found inside the crop of a Christmas goose brought to Baker Street.
Hosmer Angel's disguise was so effective that Miss Sutherland never recognized her own stepfather courting her.
Holmes once refused a knighthood for services rendered to the British Crown during a sensitive investigation.
The Blanched Soldier case is one of only two adventures narrated by Holmes himself rather than by Watson.
The Lion's Mane case was solved by Holmes in retirement when he identified a deadly jellyfish as the killer.
Wiggins is the leader of the Baker Street Irregulars who reports directly to Holmes with surveillance findings.
Holmes's method of detection relies on the science of deduction combined with minute observation of physical evidence.
The Priory School case involved the kidnapping of the Duke of Holdernesse's son by his own illegitimate half-brother.
Holmes identified the type of typewriter used to compose a letter by examining the irregularities in individual characters.
The Reigate Squires case was solved when Holmes noticed that a torn fragment of paper was written by two different hands.
Jonathan Small lost his leg to a crocodile in the Andaman Islands and was fitted with a wooden peg in its place.
Bartholomew Sholto was murdered by a poison dart fired through a ceiling hole by Tonga, Small's diminutive companion.
Holmes's rooms at Baker Street were filled with chemical apparatus, case files, and his collection of criminal relics.
The Greek Interpreter case introduced Mycroft Holmes and his remarkable ability to deduce facts about passing strangers.
Holmes once spent several days posing as a dying man to trap the murderer Culverton Smith into confessing.
The Norwood Builder case involved a man who faked his own death to frame an innocent person for murder.
Jefferson Hope drove a cab through London while systematically tracking down and poisoning Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson.
Holmes determined that Dr. Grimesby Roylott trained a venomous snake to climb down a bell-rope and kill his stepdaughters.
The Beryl Coronet case involved a banker's son who bent a national treasure to extract three valuable beryls.
Holmes kept a gasogene and a tantalus of spirits on the sideboard at Baker Street for refreshment during consultations.
The Final Problem chronicles Watson's account of Holmes and Moriarty's deadly encounter at the Reichenbach Falls.
Holmes solved the mystery of the Boscombe Valley by proving that the dying man's last word referred to an Australian city.
The stockbroker's clerk case involved a fraudster who impersonated a banking firm to rob the vault of Mawson and Williams.
Holmes maintained a vast encyclopedic knowledge of crime but claimed ignorance of basic astronomy and literature.
The cardboard box case was solved when Holmes matched two severed ears to their owner using anatomical similarities.
Thaddeus Sholto revealed to Watson and Miss Morstan the location of the Agra treasure hidden in his brother's attic.
Holmes's monograph on the dating of documents analyzed variations in watermarks, typefaces, and paper composition.
The Resident Patient Dr. Trevelyan was menaced by former associates of the Worthingdon bank robbery seeking revenge.
Holmes once disguised himself as a plumber named Escott to infiltrate Milverton's household and gather intelligence.
The Three Students case involved a Cambridge examination paper that was secretly copied before the test date.
Holmes deduced that John Turner was the real killer in the Boscombe Valley case, acting to protect his daughter's future.
Porlock was a shadowy informant within Moriarty's organization who occasionally sent coded warnings to Holmes.
The Wisteria Lodge case involved a former Central American dictator hiding under a false identity in Surrey.
Holmes stored his index of biographies in a large tin box, organized alphabetically for quick reference.
Dr. Watson's literary accounts of Holmes's cases were sometimes embellished for dramatic effect, annoying Holmes greatly.
Holmes once identified a man's profession as a retired sergeant of Marines from his bearing and tattoo alone.
The Abbey Grange case involved a woman who conspired with her lover to kill her abusive husband and staged a burglary.
Holmes decoded the message RACHE written in blood as the German word for revenge, not the name Rachel.
The Illustrious Client Sir James Damery hired Holmes to prevent a young woman from marrying the murderous Baron Gruner.
Baron Gruner kept a leather-bound diary cataloguing his conquests of women, which was used to expose him.
The Retired Colourman case involved a man who murdered his wife and her lover using gas piped into a sealed room.
Holmes examined the depth to which parsley had sunk into butter on a hot day to determine a meal's timing.
Stamford introduced Watson to Holmes at the chemical laboratory of St. Bartholomew's Hospital in London.
Holmes identified over a hundred and sixty separate ciphers in his monograph and could decode most at a glance.
The Devil's Foot root produced a toxic vapor that killed one woman and drove two men permanently insane in Cornwall.
The crooked man Henry Wood returned after decades of presumed death to confront Colonel Barclay, who had betrayed him.
The Six Napoleons were plaster busts systematically smashed by a thief searching for the black pearl of the Borgias.
Holmes's magnifying glass and measuring tape were his most essential field tools for examining crime scenes.
The Dying Detective ruse succeeded when Culverton Smith unwittingly confessed to murder in front of hidden witnesses.
The Copper Beeches governess Violet Hunter was hired specifically because she resembled Rucastle's imprisoned daughter.
Holmes hung his correspondence on the mantelpiece with a jack-knife and kept his cigars in the coal scuttle.
The Giant Rat of Sumatra is a case so terrible that Watson declared the world was not yet prepared to hear it.
Holmes used the Bertillon system of physical measurements alongside his own methods to identify criminal suspects.
The Red Circle case involved an Italian secret society called the Carbonari operating within London's immigrant community.
